data.title: Vulcan Materials: A Good Buy At Current Levels
data.description:
Investment Thesis

Vulcan Materials Company's (NYSE:VMC) revenue should benefit from continued strength in public infrastructure markets, data centres, and heavy manufacturing. In addition, the warehousing market is also close to the bottom and seeing some green shoots. This should help the company more than offset some headwinds in residential and light non-residential due to the high interest rate environment. The pricing is also expected to be strong, with the company benefiting from the carry-over impact of mid-year price increases from last year, as well as price increases from earlier this year. In addition, the company has reasonable net leverage, and I am expecting bolt-on M&As to complement organic growth.

Margin should also benefit from price increases and cost optimization through the "Vulcan Way of Operating." The valuation is lower than historical, and I believe the stock is a good buy at current levels.

Revenue Analysis and Outlook

After seeing good growth post-COVID, VMC's revenue growth was negatively impacted last year by elevated interest rates, which resulted in softer demand from the private construction sector, including residential and nonresidential (primarily warehouses) markets. However, a favorable pricing environment and continued growth in public construction activity (infrastructure markets) supported revenue growth last quarter.

In the fourth quarter of 2024, the company's revenues grew by 1.1% Y/Y to $1.853 billion. In the Aggregates segment, revenues increased by 4.2% Y/Y, driven by price increases, higher shipments for the public construction market, and strong demand in the storm-impacted areas of Western North Carolina and East Tennessee, partially offset by reduced private construction activity. Aggregates freight-adjusted selling prices were up 11% Y/Y, while the shipments decreased by 3% Y/Y. The Asphalt segment's revenues increased by 14.2% Y/Y, attributed to price increases and higher shipments. However, the Concrete segment's revenues were down 36.1% Y/Y due to tough Y/Y comps, as the prior year included revenues from the divested concrete operations in Texas (divested in November 2023).

Moving forward, the company is set to report its Q1 results on April 30th, and I am optimistic about the company's growth prospects. The company derives 40% of its revenue from public markets, which are seeing a good demand trend thanks to continued Infrastructure Investment and Jobs Act (IIJA) related spending. If we look at commentary by some of the other companies like United Rentals (URI) exposed to the infrastructure end market, out of more than $500 billion earmarked for infrastructure projects under IIJA, only $200 billion has been allocated, and $300 billion is still remaining to be assigned. Further, out of $200 billion allocated, only a fraction has been spent. So, there is a strong runaway for growth in the infrastructure market moving forward.

Additionally, $45 billion of funding was passed at the state and local level in the recent election cycle, which should help transportation infrastructure spending in the coming years. On its last earnings call, management noted that trailing 12 months highway starts increased by $7 billion to $122 billion, which should continue to drive aggregates demand for transportation projects in the coming years.

On the private market side, while there is some pressure due to the high interest rate environment, the robust Data Center activity and reshoring activity are helping the company offset it to a good extent. On the last earnings call, management cited co-star data, according to which, approximately 7% of proposed data center activity is within 20 miles of a Vulcan facility. This positions VMC well to capitalize on Data Center construction demand.

Management is also seeing some green shoots in the warehousing market and noted that warehouse starts have been flattening out (vs prior decline) on a trailing twelve-month basis and have turned positive on a trailing three-month basis, indicating we are close to the bottom and can see a potential rebound in the medium term. The warehouse market is a key driver for aggregate demand, and its bottoming out bodes well for the company from a medium to long-term perspective.

Residential and light non-residential markets continue to be weak due to the high interest rate environment. However, the interest rate cycle has started reversing, and I am expecting more cuts moving forward as the Federal Reserve takes steps to mitigate any potential slowdown caused by Trump Tariffs. This should drive recovery in these markets in the medium to long run.

Overall, I expect good public infrastructure demand, strength in data centers, and potential recovery in warehousing to more than offset near-term softness in the Residential and Light non-residential market, resulting in slightly positive organic aggregate volume.

The pricing environment also remains healthy. The company has posted good pricing increases over the last few years, and for the current year, management has guided for freight-adjusted aggregate pricing growth of between 5% and 7% despite a 100 bps negative mix impact from recent acquisitions.

VMC's CEO Tom Hill also indicated that the company will announce mid-year price increases, which are not included in the guidance given at the time of the last earnings call. So, that can result in upside to the pricing guidance for FY2025 as well, though most of the impact from mid-year increases should be felt in 2026. Below is the relevant excerpt:

... So they are not included in our guide, but we will absolutely announce midyear price increases. We will announce those probably towards the end of the first quarter, so we got time down those conversations."

- Tom Hill, Chairman and CEO, Vulcan Materials on Q42024 Earnings Call

So, flattish to slightly positive volume growth coupled with a healthy pricing increase in aggregates should help the company achieve good organic revenue growth.

In addition to organic growth, the company's FY25 revenues should also benefit from a full-year contribution from the 2024 acquisition. The company also had a reasonable net leverage of 2.3x at the end of the last year. So, there are also good chances of further bolt-on M&As in FY25.

Overall, I remain optimistic about the company's growth prospects in the near as well as long term.

Margin Analysis and Outlook

In Q4 2024, the company's margins benefited from price increases, operational efficiencies, and moderating inflationary input costs. As a result, the gross margin expanded by 330 bps Y/Y to 29%, and the adjusted EBITDA margin was up 370 bps Y/Y to 29.7%.

Segment-wise, the gross margin increased by 300 bps Y/Y in the Aggregates segment and 140 bps Y/Y in the Asphalt segment. In the Concrete segment, gross margin declined by 170 bps Y/Y, driven by the divestiture of the concrete operations in Texas, partially offset by price increases.

Moving forward, I am optimistic about the company's margin growth prospects. The company has seen moderation in inflationary cost pressures in the past few quarters. For FY25, management has guided for a 5% to 7% increase in freight-adjusted aggregate pricing and a low to mid single-digit increase in freight-adjusted aggregate unit cash cost. So, price/cost is expected to be positive moving forward. The company is not directly exposed to Trump Tariffs in a meaningful way. So, compared to other industries, it is better placed. The company is benefitting from the "Vulcan way of operating," where it is focusing on improving training, operator skills, equipment inspection, and leveraging technology for better throughput and reduced downtimes, which should continue to help it deliver incremental savings in the medium to long run. This, coupled with positive pricing, bodes well for a longer-term margin profile.

Valuation

VMC is trading at 28.88x FY25 consensus EPS estimates of $8.39 and 24.77X FY26 consensus EPS estimates of $9.78. This is a discount versus its 5-year historical average P/E (forward) of 31.79x.

While the company's forward P/E is higher than its peer Martin Marietta Materials (MLM), which is trading at 26.39x FY25 P/E and 22.46x FY26 P/E, its forward EV/EBITDA of 15.12x is almost in line with MLM's 15.54x.

I believe we are closer to the bottom in residential and light non-residential business, and as interest rates continue turning lower, these end markets should start recovering. This, coupled with strength in public infrastructure, heavy manufacturing (reshoring-related), and data centre end markets, and potential recovery in warehousing, bodes well for growth acceleration in the coming years. The company has already implemented healthy pricing increases in recent years. With the demand environment improving further in the medium term, pricing increases may further surprise on the upside. Given a good amount of pricing flows due to the bottom line, we should see further margin expansion and earnings growth acceleration.

Overall, VMC is a good long-term compounder to have in a portfolio. The stock has corrected in recent weeks despite good fundamentals and no meaningful direct exposure to tariffs, and is trading at a discount compared to historical levels. I believe management commentary at the time of the next earnings call should help ease some of the investor concerns, as the fundamental long-term growth story with pricing increases, margin improvement, and a solid demand environment in the public infrastructure space remains intact. Hence, I have a buy rating on the stock.

Risks

The company doesn't have direct exposure to tariffs. I am expecting a favourable tariff negotiation outcome, with most of the U.S. trading partners offering concessions in the form of lowered tariffs on the U.S. products. However, if these negotiations don't go as expected and result in stagflation in the U.S., the interest rate cycle reversal may be paused, and recovery in residential and light non-residential markets may not materialize. In this case, the growth acceleration I am expecting may not materialize.

In addition to organic growth, the company also pursues a bolt-on M&A strategy to drive growth. Inorganic growth is relatively risky, and there are risks related to overpaying for an acquisition or intended synergies not getting realized. If that happens, the stock price may be negatively impacted.

data.title: Microsoft: A Brilliant Business, But Here's Why It's Not In My Portfolio
data.description:
There is no doubt that Microsoft Corp. (NASDAQ:MSFT), along with OpenAI, are leading the way in the rise of AI. Yes, Nvidia (NVDA) is there as well, though they’re more in the business of selling the picks and shovels needed for AI. What I find more interesting is how AI is being used and what it’s actually helping to build. That’s where Microsoft comes in, with products like Azure OpenAI and Copilot.

The selloff in the broader markets has reshuffled the Mag 7 ranking by market cap, with Apple (AAPL) losing its leadership in the S&P 500 to Microsoft, (at the moment of writing this article) with a market cap of $2.90T and an enterprise value of $2.93T. Of course, seeing the volatility after yesterday's 10.5% jump, the #1 ranking will certainly fluctuate over the next weeks.

I admit in this introduction that analyzing Microsoft has been one of the most challenging tasks I encountered in the past years. Back in the old days (way before writing for SA), when I was a deep value investor, I wouldn't even touch anything with a mkt cap over $2 billion. Actually, that's usually where I set the upper limit on my screener. This being my initial coverage on Microsoft, I welcome constructive feedback on my thesis.

Overall, after reports came in pointing to a slowdown in Microsoft's data center buildout (although management recently reaffirmed the $80B CapEx), along with a more flexible (and, therefore, less favorable for Azure) partnership with Open AI, I don't see its premium valuation justified. So, let's start with dessert.

The Downside

Microsoft's overvaluation, when compared to its peers, was traditionally justified by its strong narrative. The company has strong ties with Open AI that trace back to 2019, which ultimately led to the Azure OpenAI service, the GitHub Copilot, and the Microsoft 365 Copilot.

This two-way partnership made perfect sense. Microsoft provides the infrastructure (data centers) to train Open AI's LLM and the tools for devs to build through Azure. OpenAI, on the other hand, brings the AI part (i.e., ChatGPT) to the devs' apps and websites.

Back in January, this partnership evolved to allow OpenAI to use cloud platforms beyond Microsoft's Azure. Initially, I wasn't concerned at all. In fact, it made sense, considering the $500 billion Stargate project. Why?

Because Open AI is starving for computing power. If they want to expand to GPT-5 and beyond, they need extra computing capacity. This means loosening some of the partnership rules with Microsoft so they can work with other data center and cloud providers.

Well, things stopped making sense after the TD Cowen report. The report was so influential that Microsoft had to step in and confirm they still plan to spend $80 billion on CapEx this year.

Isn't it interesting how, in almost any other company, investors generally want CapEx to do down in order to improve the free cash flow position of the company? Well, that is not the case for the big four hyperscalers. If their CapEx goes down, that's a signal that AI demand is not where everyone thought it would be.

According to Microsoft, their "plans to spend over $80B on infrastructure this FY remains on track as we continue to grow at a record pace to meet customer demand". However, Microsoft also said that it "may strategically pace or adjust our infrastructure in some areas."

Recently, reports came in stating that Microsoft will not be exercising a $12 billion option to buy additional data center capacity from CoreWeave. This, in addition to the slowdown of the $1 billion data center project in Ohio and others around the world, are not encouraging signs of demand for AI workloads.

Overall, I see a soft narrative that doesn't really justify its high valuation.

Valuation Comparison

The comparison analysis below includes Apple, Amazon (AMZN), Oracle (ORCL), and Google (GOOG). I believe these are the closest peers, even though their offerings might differ from Microsoft.

My discontent comes from the P/S ratio. Microsoft is trading over 3.5x Amazon on a P/S basis. That premium is often justified if the company is able to maximize sales into earnings (remember that NVIDIA is more expensive than AMD (AMD) on a P/S basis, but on a P/E basis, the difference is abysmal, 39 vs 96, respectively).

Therefore, seeing Microsoft's P/E in line with its peers is not particularly encouraging (at least, not to me). If the company traders at a premium on P/S, I want to see a discount on a P/E basis.

Even less encouraging is the P/CF ratio, which is the second highest in the group.

Overall, I can't say that Microsoft is a cheap company. That said, Microsoft has always been expensive, and the recent selloff has cooled off its valuation ratios, some of them now trading below their median.

The Upside

So far, Microsoft's AI business is showing strong signs of growth, with an annual revenue run rate in the past quarter of $13 billion, up 175% yoy.

Naturally, this led to the Azure and other cloud services revenue (see below) growth of 31% yoy in the past quarter.

According to the Q2 FY25 earnings call, a big part of Azure's growth came from AI services. Specifically, AI services made up 13 percentage points of the total 31 percent increase. However, I wouldn't pop a bottle of champagne just yet, as the growth seems to have slowed down since Q1 2024.

One metric that definitely is taking off is the commercial bookings growth. As seen below, the trend is quite encouraging, with a 67% yoy increase in the past quarter.

Furthermore, if we were to consider a constant currency basis (remember from the previous section that only half of Microsoft's revenue is generated in the US), the growth in the past quarter would have been 75%. Not bad. This ensures that revenue growth will continue its double-digit trajectory over the next few years. At the very least, until the partnership with Open AI comes to an end in 2030. Isn't it interesting that the Street's projections for revenue growth drop after 2030? (It's not a coincidence, in my view)

In fact, when asked during the Q&A, CFO Amy Hood said that most of the bookings growth was "driven by Azure commitments from OpenAI."

Looking at the financial statements, I have absolutely no complaints. The company looks healthy, with double-digit earnings and operating cash flow growth. Free cash flow is the only metric that is showing a clear deterioration, although, as I said before, this is positively welcomed by investors, as the increase in CapEx is a direct sign that demand for AI is solid. Speaking of CapEx, the estimate for this year is $80 billion, below Amazon's $100 billion but above Alphabet's $75 billion and Meta's $60 - $65 billion.

Other than this, Microsoft is seeing strong demand for Copilot. This is reflected in the 16% yoy increase in Microsoft 365's commercial revenue. Additionally, the number of consumer subscribers is still increasing, which is a reflection of how sticky Microsoft 365 is once you have adopted the ecosystem.

Conclusion

To wrap up, I have a high conviction that Microsoft remains a strong company at the heart of the AI expansion cycle. In the past quarter, Azure revenue increased by 31% yoy, mainly driven by AI services, contributing 13 points. That said, at a P/S ratio over 3.5 times Amazon and a P/CF second highest among its peers, the valuation leaves little room for error.

On the positive side, the company has reaffirmed its CapEx guidance of $80 billion this year. However, recent pullbacks, like walking away from a $12 billion CoreWeave option or slowing down the Ohio $1B data center project, raise questions as to how high the demand for AI workload is.

Unless earnings accelerate meaningfully, it’s hard to justify paying 34x forward earnings. At $2.9 trillion in market cap, the upside looks quite limited to me.
